retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Opportunistic Infections in HIV/AIDS -- Function -- Strategies to prevent opportunistic infections in HIV/AIDS:
retrieved_docs.content: Prevention of opportunistic infections in HIV/AIDS involves a multidisciplinary approach that includes primary care physicians, infectious disease agents, and social workers for support. Early initiation of HAART remains the most efficient method for preventing OI. [9] [10] [11] Additional methods include the felicitous application of several strategies including vaccinations, screenings for co-infections, avoidance of exposures, patient education, and antibiotic prophylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Staging -- Stage 0 or Primary HIV Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Patients have 1 or more symptoms associated with acute HIV or a constellation of symptoms consistent with acute retroviral syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Opportunistic Infections in HIV/AIDS -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The timely introduction of ART to improved CD4+ counts is the most efficient method of minimizing the risk of opportunistic infections. The use of ART can however lead to IRIS due to inflammatory response once improvement of CD4+ cell count is seen and immune response has been achieved. The presentation of IRIS can be nonspecific and depends on the type of opportunistic infection, location, and severity of inflammatory response. Due to its nonspecific presentation, it can be hard to differentiate it from active infections (e.g hospital-acquired pneumonia), a progression of previously diagnosed opportunistic infections, or drug-related side effects. Continuation of ART and symptomatic management is the cornerstone of managing IRIS. The use of steroids is reserved for severe forms of IRIS. [50] [51] [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Opportunistic Infections in HIV/AIDS -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: HIV patients who are untreated are prone to multiple opportunistic infections due to the progressive lowering of their CD4 + T cells. Because of the high morbidity and mortality of these infections, every attempt should be made that these patients have enough resources for early diagnosis of HIV, timely initiation of ART, regular follow up to ensure medication adherence.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS (Nursing) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Some patients may complain of nonspecific symptoms at the time of primary/acute HIV infection. [1] Most patients do not seek medical attention for these symptoms and remain undiagnosed. They subsequently enter a long chronic asymptomatic phase of infection, which can last for decades. [2] The end stage of the disease is defined by severe invasive opportunistic infections or HIV-associated malignancies that are termed AIDS-defining lesions. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Opportunistic Infections in HIV/AIDS -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the pathophysiology and risk factors of opportunistic infections. Describe the role of antiviral therapy, vaccinations and immunizations in the prevention of opportunistic infections. Identify the development of immune reconstitution syndrome after initiation of HAART. Outline the common opportunistic infections in HIV patients, risk factors for exposure and prophylactic antibiotics. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Determine the stage of HIV and related comorbidities to ensure timely diagnosis and treatment for patients. Apply the principles of antiretroviral therapy and prophylaxis for opportunistic infections to prevent and manage adverse effects. Interpret HIV treatment guidelines to ensure cultural sensitivity and maintain confidentiality for individual patients and affected populations. Collaborate with interprofessional team members to ensure continuity of care, improve patient outcomes for HIV prevention and treatment, and mitigate the risk of AIDS. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Opportunistic Infections in HIV/AIDS -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Opportunistic infections (OI) are a significant cause of morbidity and mortality in patients with HIV/AIDS. Although the incidence of OI has reduced since the introduction of highly active antiretroviral therapy (ART) in 1995, it continues to add to the burden of HIV-related hospitalizations and deaths in patients. This is particularly true in patients who have not received HAART therapy or who have been non-compliant to it. [1] [2] These infections generally lack the virulence and pathogenicity to cause disease in immunocompetent hosts and are hence an uncommon phenomenon. [3] However, due to the immunodeficiency, the risk of OI in HIV/AIDS is substantially increased, particularly in the absence of ART and antimicrobial prophylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS (Nursing) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is an enveloped retrovirus that contains two copies of a single-stranded RNA genome. Without treatment, all patients with an HIV infection develop severe immunosuppression, which causes multiple opportunistic infections and HIV-associated malignancies. The last stage of an HIV infection is called acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), which is invariably fatal.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate opportunistic infections according to the CD4+ levels in patients with human immunodeficiency virus. Identify the signs and symptoms of opportunistic infections in patients with human immunodeficiency virus. Explain the management of opportunistic infections in patients with human immunodeficiency virus. Explain the importance of improving care coordination amongst interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients with opportunistic infections concurrent with human immunodeficiency virus. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Treatment / Management -- Opportunistic Infections
retrieved_docs.content: Toxoplasma gondii :  Patients with CD4+ counts less than 100 cells/mm 3 who have positive test results for toxoplasma antibodies require chemoprophylaxis . [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be educated on the transmission of HIV, how the virus is acquired, and how it can spread to others. Education regarding the CD4 count level is necessary, as well as the role ART plays in maintaining the patient’s immune function and the side effects of the medications.  Patients should be made aware of the opportunistic infections, malignancies, and comorbid conditions that may occur with long-term HIV infection, AIDS, and ART.  Emphasis should be on the fact that with proper medical care and ART, many patients with HIV can lead very normal lives for many years after diagnosis, which just a few decades ago was not the case for this disease. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- History and Physical -- Acute HIV
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 90% of patients with acute HIV experience at least 1 symptom within the first 4 weeks after primary HIV infection. These symptoms are typically mild, nonspecific, and self-limited. Some patients present with more severe symptoms, known as acute retroviral syndrome or seroconversion illness. These symptoms are listed below in order of decreasing frequency. Fever Fatigue Muscle pain Skin rash Headache Sore throat Swollen lymph nodes Joint pain Night sweats Diarrhea

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- History and Physical -- AIDS
retrieved_docs.content: Eventually, HIV progresses to advanced disease, particularly if left untreated or inadequately treated. AIDS is diagnosed when specific AIDS-defining conditions are noted, regardless of the CD4+ count. These AIDS-defining conditions, outlined by the CDC, include: Candidiasis of the digestive tract (other than thrush) Candidiasis of the pulmonary tract Invasive cervical cancer Extrapulmonary or disseminated coccidioidomycosis, histoplasmosis, or cryptococcosis, including cryptococcal meningitis Chronic intestinal cryptosporidiosis or isosporiasis Cytomegalovirus retinitis Kaposi sarcoma HIV encephalitis and HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder Tuberculosis Primary lymphoma of the brain Non-Hodgkin lymphoma Burkitt lymphoma Mycobacterial infections Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy Salmonella septicemia HIV-associated wasting syndrome [19]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of opportunistic infections in AIDS remains poor; however, improving patient compliance with ART may lead to fewer infections, in turn improving overall outcomes in HIV positive patients. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- History and Physical -- Chronic HIV
retrieved_docs.content: After HIV acquisition and subsequent setting of the viral set point, patients enter the chronic phase of the infection. Most patients with chronic HIV remain asymptomatic before developing AIDS. However, nonspecific fatigue may present, and persistent generalized lymphadenopathy is usual. Generalized lymphadenopathy is characterized by at least 2 noncontiguous sites other than inguinal nodes exhibiting enlarged lymph nodes for more than 3 to 6 months, not explained by other lymphoproliferative or infectious causes. [4] [12] Patients with chronic HIV without AIDS can develop oropharyngeal candidiasis, recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis, oral hairy leukoplakia, disseminated cutaneous herpes simplex virus, and cervical dysplasia or cervical carcinoma in situ. [13] [14] [15] Cutaneous manifestations such as seborrheic dermatitis, bacillary angiomatosis, varicella-zoster virus reactivations, and molluscum contagiosum infections are common and tend to be severe in patients with HIV. [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 0 -- Community 21
retrieved_docs.content: these entities and relationships delineate a comprehensive framework of antiviral, antiretroviral, and antifungal drug classes, their structural families, mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, epidemiologic links, and relevant contraindications, thereby informing optimal clinical selection, prevention, and treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic approach labeled “Allowed qualifier” is indicated for a range of conditions, including acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة), various injury‑ and infection‑related cases (حالات الإصابات والعدوى), chronic fungal infection (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ), and pathological adhesion (الالتصاق). This therapy is therefore applicable across both immunologic, infectious, and tissue‑related disorders, highlighting its broad relevance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: HIV is a chronic viral infection that can be managed with antiretroviral therapy, and tenofovir disoproxil is an antiretroviral prodrug that may be used to treat HIV infection. Tenofovir disoproxil works by inhibiting viral replication, thereby helping to suppress HIV viral load, preserve immune function, and reduce disease progression, making it a key component of comprehensive HIV patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: preventive and therapeutic use enables precise prescribing, optimized patient care, and effective public‑health interventions across viral diseases such as HIV, ocular viral infections, influenza, smallpox, and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1878
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a major route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdine‑mesylate and efavirenz have the potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in prophylactic strategies. In contrast, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in individuals with HIV infection, indicating that this immunosuppressive medication should be avoided in this patient population. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in both preventing HIV transmission and selecting safe therapeutic options for patients living with the virus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1211
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. In patients with HIV infection, the use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated, reflecting the need to avoid this immunosuppressive agent in this population. Conversely, the antiretroviral drugs delavirdin and delavirdin‑mesylate are noted for their potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in prophylactic strategies. Understanding these relationships—risk behaviors, drug contraindications, and preventive therapies—is essential for effective patient management and infection control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, a major route of transmission. In the context of HIV prevention and therapy, delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir are identified as agents that may help prevent acquisition of the virus, while fostemsavir is recognized for its potential to treat established HIV infection. Conversely, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in individuals with HIV infection, indicating that this medication should be avoided in this patient population. Understanding these relationships supports informed decision‑making in both preventive strategies and therapeutic management of HIV‑related care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1181
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFECTIVES FOR SYSTEMIC USE is a broad therapeutic class that includes both ANTIVIRALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE and antimycotics for systemic use. These subcategories represent systemic agents specifically targeting viral infections and fungal infections, respectively, and together they constitute the systemic anti‑infective armamentarium used in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
retrieved_docs.content: the importance of recognizing contraindicated drug classes, and the role of antiviral agents such as zanamivir in preventing viral respiratory illnesses that can lead to secondary bacterial or fungal pneumonias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: illustrate how antiviral, antiretroviral, and antifungal drugs are organized into mechanistic ATC classes—direct‑acting antivirals, other antivirals, entry inhibitors, integrase inhibitors, NNRTIs, NRTIs, non‑RT nucleoside analogues, neuraminidase inhibitors, and echinocandins—and how specific agents within each class target distinct viral or fungal processes. The relationships also link drugs to preventive or therapeutic indications (HIV prevention, HIV treatment, hepatitis C, hepatitis B/D, influenza, smallpox, viral infection prophylaxis) and to epidemiologic contexts such as HIV infection (a viral infection associated with shared needle use) and influenza‑related pneumonia, thereby supporting informed therapeutic decision‑making and public‑health control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 576
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir / lamivudine is a combination antiviral medication that is classified as a member of the group “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2665
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, and etravirine have been identified as medications that may prevent HIV infection, highlighting their potential role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader infection‑control strategies. Understanding both the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use and the preventive capabilities of these drugs is essential for effective patient counseling, risk reduction, and public‑health interventions aimed at curbing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1025
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely associated with the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. In patients with HIV infection, the use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated because of potential adverse interactions. Several antiretroviral agents have preventive activity against HIV infection, including delavirdine‑mesylate, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir. In addition to prevention, cabotegravir and fostemsavir are employed as therapeutic agents for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the Integrase Inhibitors class of systemic anti‑infectives, reflecting their shared mechanism of targeting the viral integrase enzyme. This network of transmission risk, drug contraindications, preventive agents, and treatment options underscores the importance of integrated management strategies for individuals at risk for or living with HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 0 -- Community 5
retrieved_docs.content: the antifungal ATC category “Other antimycotics for systemic use,” the echinocandin drugs kaspofungin (a lipopeptidy compound) and micafungin are listed. HIV infection is categorized as a viral infection primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents—including NRTIs, NNRTIs, INSTIs, entry, attachment, fusion and protease inhibitors, as well as monoclonal antibodies—is employed for both treatment and prophylaxis. This hierarchical organization of drug classes, individual agents, chemical families and disease indications provides a comprehensive framework for understanding antiviral, antifungal and antibacterial therapies and their clinical applications.
